title: DAVID — Old English: Beowulf's Tongue in Manuscript

gate_0.music_plan: original score only — music cleared, cue sheet on file

brand.title: DAVID · The Archive
brand.subtitle: Dead languages, actually pronounced.
brand.cta: Bring the dead tongues back — one attestation at a time. DAVID.

beats.speech_text: I am DAVID. Today, Old English — the speech of Beowulf and Alfred, preserved in ink that outlasted the kingdoms where it was spoken.
Norman conquest reshaped England's tongue. Old English survived only in manuscripts — one fire away from oblivion, as the Cotton library proved.
What does a single surviving poem tell us about how a language sounded — when even its spelling fights the modern eye?
The Beowulf manuscript, the West Saxon Gospels, and charters with contemporary spellings give us thorn, eth, and vowel graphs — plus alliteration that demands stress patterns.

We read West Saxon graphs against Mercian variants, use alliterative meter to test stress, and consult comparative Germanic and later Middle English spellings — never smoothing away dialect difference without saying so.
The manuscript forms are attested. Short vowels in unstressed syllables and some diphthong timings are reconstructed from comparative evidence — labeled on screen.
Listen — Hwaet, we gar-Dena in geardagum — So, we Spear-Danes in days of yore — opening line of Beowulf, West Saxon stress and reconstructed vowels.
Stay with the Archive — and hear English before it became the English you speak today.
